title: System Overview
body:
System Overview

What this is, how it works, and where your data lives.

This is a purpose-built, browser-based productivity system grounded in David Allen's Getting Things Done (GTD) methodology. It runs entirely as a single HTML file hosted on GitHub Pages — no server, no login, no subscription — and stores all data in a Google Drive JSON file that you own.

Two files, that's it

  • index.html — the full application (UI, logic, and layout), hosted at kpickhardt-gtd.github.io/gtd/.
  • gtd-cloud-state.json — your data. All tasks, projects, notes, and log entries live here, stored in a Google Drive folder you own.
Key principle: Your data is yours. No account required beyond Google, no third-party servers, no sync fees. Everything lives in a Drive folder you control and is readable as plain text.

Cloud sync

The app connects to Google Drive via the Drive REST API. Click 🔗 Connect via Google Drive (top-right), sign in with Google, and paste your Drive folder ID when prompted. Changes save automatically within seconds. The app polls Drive every 30 seconds so multiple devices stay in sync.

Reconnecting

Google access tokens live in memory and expire when you close the browser. On your next visit, click 🔗 Connect — it signs you back in with one click (no folder re-entry needed) and pushes any changes made while offline to Drive. Your data is always preserved locally between sessions.

Recommended browser

Any modern browser — Chrome, Edge, Firefox, Safari. Works on desktop and mobile.

title: The GTD Model
body:
The GTD Model

The methodology behind the system — and why it works.

GTD is a personal productivity system created by David Allen. The core idea: your brain is for having ideas, not holding them. Everything that requires your attention gets captured outside your head, clarified, and placed in a trusted system. You then review that system regularly to decide what to act on.

The system implements GTD's six canonical lists, a structured capture/clarify workflow, and a three-cadence review system.

The promise: When your system is current and trusted, you can engage fully with whatever you're doing — because nothing is falling through the cracks.

title: The Six Lists
body:
The Six Lists

What each tab is for and what belongs in it.

Inbox

Where everything lands first. Capture without judging or sorting — get it out of your head. The Inbox is meant to be processed to zero regularly, not stored in permanently. Use Quick Capture (the bar at the top of every tab) to toss anything in mid-thought.

Next Actions

Every discrete, physical action you're committed to doing. Each item should start with a verb and be specific enough to start without further thought.

  • Context — where/how it's done: @computer @phone @email @meeting @ask @draft @think @errands @home @office
  • Project — the parent project (optional for standalone actions)
  • Priority — High ↑, Medium ·, or Low ↓
  • Energy — Quick (<15 min), Focused (30–60 min), or Deep (1+ hr)
  • Focus Date — the date you intend to work on this; items set to today appear in Dashboard suggestions
  • Due Date — hard deadline, shown with a warning when overdue

Projects

Any desired outcome requiring more than one action step. Projects don't get "done" directly — they get done by completing their Next Actions. Every active project should always have at least one Next Action assigned. Projects without one are "stalled" and flagged automatically on the Dashboard and Projects list.

Waiting For

Things you've delegated or are expecting from others. Records who you're waiting on and when. Review regularly — if something is overdue, send a nudge.

Calendar

Time-specific commitments — meetings, deadlines, appointments. Unlike Next Actions, calendar items have a fixed date and cannot be rescheduled without a reason.

Someday / Maybe

A parking lot for things you're interested in but not committed to yet. Review monthly to promote ready items to active projects or prune dead ones.

Areas of Focus

Ongoing responsibilities and roles that never end — "Leadership," "Client Relationships," "Personal Health." Use these as a lens during the Weekly Review: is anything being neglected?

title: Capture & Clarify
body:
Capture & Clarify Workflow

How to process your Inbox — the core GTD loop.

  1. Capture — use Quick Capture to toss anything into the Inbox without stopping to think.
  2. Clarify — during your review, open each inbox item and ask: Is it actionable?
  3. If yes: Is it one step or many? One step → Next Action. Many steps → Project (with a first Next Action assigned). If it takes under 2 minutes, do it immediately.
  4. If no: Is it reference (archive in notes), someday/maybe (move to Someday), or trash (delete)?
  5. Repeat until the Inbox is empty.
Important: The Inbox is not a to-do list. Items in the Inbox have not been clarified. Until something is moved to Next Actions, it has no committed place in your system and won't appear in priority suggestions.

Processing tips

  • Don't skip items — process every inbox entry, even if only to delete it.
  • The 2-minute rule: if an action takes less than 2 minutes, do it now instead of adding it to the system.
  • One pass, no lingering — make a decision on each item and move on. Don't re-read items without deciding.

title: Review Cadences
body:
Review Cadences

The Review tab has structured checklists for all three cadences.

Daily Review (5–10 min)

  • Scan calendar for today and tomorrow
  • Triage Inbox to zero
  • Pick 1–3 priority Next Actions for today (set their Focus Date to today)
  • Glance at Waiting For — any nudges to send?
  • Capture anything still rattling around in your head

Weekly Review (30–60 min — recommended Fridays)

The cornerstone of GTD. The system's checklist follows Allen's three-step structure:

  • Get Clear — empty Inbox to zero; process all loose papers, emails, and notes
  • Get Current — review and update each list (Next Actions, Calendar, Projects, Waiting For, Areas of Focus)
  • Get Creative — review Someday/Maybe; capture new ideas and opportunities

Monthly Review (60+ min)

  • Review Areas of Focus — are they still the right ones?
  • Review Someday/Maybe in full — promote ready items, archive dead ones
  • Review longer-horizon goals (1-year, 3-year)
  • Audit where your time actually went
  • Identify one thing to drop, one thing to double down on
The rule: GTD lives or dies on the review. A system you don't trust is worse than no system — you'll carry the mental overhead anyway. The weekly review is what keeps the system current and trusted.

title: AI Integration
body:
AI Integration

How Claude works alongside this system — and how to use it well.

This system is designed to be used alongside Claude (Anthropic's AI), running in Cowork mode. Claude can read and write to the same data files you use, enabling a collaborative workflow that goes well beyond a typical task manager.

The Session Log is the bridge

The Session Log tab is a timestamped journal where both you and Claude can write entries. Both authors' entries are labeled and visually distinct (AI badge for Claude). Claude reads recent log entries at the start of a session to re-establish context — it doesn't have memory between conversations by default.

Best practice: Write log entries like you're briefing a colleague who just walked in. Include what was decided and why, not just what was done. Claude can infer task completion from the data — it needs the reasoning from you.

What Claude can do

  • Read your full task system and identify your top focus items for the day
  • Set focus dates on Next Actions and move Inbox items into the right list
  • Detect stalled projects and suggest what the first next action should be
  • Add entries to the Session Log (handoff notes, decisions, reviews)
  • Repair the cloud state file if it becomes corrupted
  • Make targeted edits to this interface when you request new features

Session handoff pattern

  1. At the end of a work session, ask Claude to write a brief Session Log entry summarizing what was worked on and what's next.
  2. At the start of the next session, Claude reads recent log entries and the current data file to re-establish context.
  3. Direct Claude to help: "What are my stalled high-priority projects?" or "Flag my top 5 focus items for tomorrow."

title: Dashboard & Focus
body:
Dashboard & Focus

Your command center — counts, suggestions, and what needs attention.

Stats row

The top of the Dashboard shows live counts for every list: Inbox, Next Actions, Projects, Waiting For, Calendar, Someday, Areas. Click any count to jump to that tab.

Suggested focus today

Automatically surfaces Next Actions that have today's date set as their Focus Date, plus any High priority actions. This is your recommended shortlist for the day. To add an item here, open it in Next Actions and set the Focus Date to today, or ask Claude: "Flag my top priorities for today."

Stalled / Needs attention

Active projects with no open Next Action are listed here. You can filter by priority (All / ↑ High / · Med / ↓ Low) to focus on what matters most. Click the item ID badge to jump directly to that project.

Rule of thumb: If a High-priority project is stalled, that's your first agenda item. Either add a next action or move the project to Blocked with a note explaining what's in the way.

title: Key Features
body:
Key Features

Filters, linking, quick capture, and other things worth knowing.

Quick Capture

The capture bar at the top of every tab sends anything straight to your Inbox on Enter. Zero friction — don't stop to categorize. Capture first, clarify during your next review.

Filters & search

Every list has full-text search. Next Actions additionally support multi-dimensional filtering: context, priority, energy, and sort order. Projects can be filtered by Area of Focus and by stalled status.

Linking items

Next Actions, Waiting For items, and Calendar entries can all be linked to a project. The system surfaces these links automatically — a project card shows how many open next actions it has, and stalled projects are flagged everywhere. When adding a Next Action, you can create a new project inline by selecting ＋ Create new project… from the project dropdown.

Focus dates vs. due dates

  • Focus Date — the date you intend to work on something. This is a soft target you set yourself. Items with today's focus date appear in Dashboard suggestions.
  • Due Date — a hard external deadline. Shown with a warning badge when overdue.

Waiting For → Next Action round-trip

When a Waiting For item is resolved, you can convert it back to a Next Action directly from the list. The conversion preserves provenance notes so you know where the action came from.

title: Getting Started
body:
Getting Started

Five steps to get your system live and trusted.

  1. Open the app. Go to kpickhardt-gtd.github.io/gtd/ in any browser. The app loads immediately.
  2. Connect to Google Drive. Click 🔗 Connect via Google Drive (top-right). Sign in with your Google account, then paste your Drive folder URL or ID. See the Google Drive Setup section for detailed steps.
  3. Clear your Inbox. Go to the Inbox tab and process every item: move actionable ones to Next Actions or Projects, delete the rest.
  4. Pick your focus. Go to Next Actions and set Focus Dates on the 3–5 things you want to work on today.
  5. Clear stalled projects. Check the Dashboard. Any high-priority stalled projects need a next action now — add one or move the project to Blocked.
Tip: Don't try to load the system perfectly on day one. Capture everything in the Inbox, run a quick review, and build the habit of daily capture + weekly review. The system gets more powerful as your data reflects your real commitments.

title: Google Drive Setup
body:
Google Drive Setup

How to connect, reconnect, and use Google Drive Desktop for AI co-editing.

First-time connection

  1. Click 🔗 Connect via Google Drive in the top-right header.
  2. Sign in with your Google account when prompted.
  3. Open Google Drive in another tab, navigate to the folder where you want your task list to live, and copy the URL from the address bar. It will look like: https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1GaCEJnWmla1gkj3...
  4. Paste the full URL (or just the folder ID after /folders/) into the dialog and click Connect.
  5. The app will verify the folder and either load your existing gtd-cloud-state.json file or offer to create a new one.
One folder = one task list. Each browser window can connect to a different Drive folder, making it possible to maintain separate lists (e.g. work vs. personal) in the same app.

Reconnecting after closing the browser

Google access tokens are in-memory only — they don't survive a browser close. The next time you open the app:

  1. Your data loads from the local cache automatically. You'll see a status message: "Drive session expired — click Connect to reconnect."
  2. Click 🔗 Connect. Google's sign-in popup appears briefly and auto-closes.
  3. The app reconnects instantly — no folder re-entry required. If you made any changes while offline, they are pushed to Drive automatically.

Setting up Google Drive Desktop for AI co-editing

If you use an AI assistant (such as Claude in Cowork mode) to help manage your task list, you can give it direct read/write access to your gtd-cloud-state.json file by mapping Google Drive as a local folder.

  1. Install Google Drive for Desktop from drive.google.com/drive/downloads. Sign in with the same Google account you use for the app.
  2. Google Drive will appear as a local drive on your computer (e.g. G:\My Drive\ on Windows). Your task list file will be at the path shown in your Drive Desktop settings.
  3. In Claude Cowork: connect the Google Drive folder by granting folder access in the session. Claude can then read and write gtd-cloud-state.json directly.
  4. Changes Claude makes to the file are picked up by Google Drive for Desktop within seconds and synced to the cloud. The app loads them on its next poll.
Conflict avoidance tip: Don't ask Claude to edit the file at the same moment you're actively using the app. Let one finish before triggering the other. If Google Drive creates a conflict file (e.g. gtd-cloud-state (1).json), simply delete the duplicate — your data is in the original.

Using multiple devices

Every browser on every device connects to the same Drive folder. Simply open the app URL, click Connect, and your full task list loads from Drive. No installation required beyond a browser.

title: Quick Reference
body:
Quick Reference

Contexts, priorities, energy levels, and the stalled rule.

Context tags

Tag each Next Action with where or how it can be done. Use context filters to batch similar actions when you're in the right mode.

  • @computer — requires a laptop or desktop
  • @phone — a call or voicemail
  • @email — an email to write or respond to
  • @meeting — agenda item for an upcoming meeting
  • @ask — a question for a specific person (in-person)
  • @draft — writing, outlining, or composition
  • @think — a decision or reflection requiring focused thought
  • @errands — requires physical presence outside the office
  • @home — done at home
  • @office — requires physical presence in the office

Priority

  • ↑ High — must get done; appears first and in Dashboard suggestions
  • · Medium — should get done this week
  • ↓ Low — nice to do; review monthly

Energy

  • Quick (<15 min) — do when you have a small window
  • Focused (30–60 min) — needs uninterrupted attention
  • Deep (1+ hr) — substantial creative or analytical work; protect this time

The stalled project rule

A project is stalled when it has no open Next Action assigned to it. GTD requires every active project to have at least one committed next action at all times. If a project is stalled, either:

  • Add a next action for it now, or
  • Move it to Blocked or Someday if you're not actively working on it
